metadata.purpose: Shelf, bracket, crust, and other primarily wood-inhabiting poroid fungi.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Hard woody bracket fungus with a white pore surface that bruises brown and can be scratched for drawing.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional/reishi-adjacent interest exists in some sources, but this guide treats it mainly as a non-food identification and practical-use species.
records.culinary_uses: Not a food mushroom; woody, perennial bracket.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.general_notes: Best treated as a shelf fungus ID entry rather than a food species.
records.foraging_value: Other use — excellent craft/tinder curiosity; not food
records.other_uses: Classic artist's conk: the fresh white pore surface bruises brown and can be scratched to make durable drawings. Hard dried brackets are also useful as teaching/display specimens.
records.field_identification: Hard woody shelf on hardwoods; dull zoned upper surface; white pore surface stains brown where scratched.
records.mushroom_card_note: Useful as an artist's conk, not as food: scratch the white pore surface to make brown drawings.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Ganoderma applanatum
use_links.notes: Poroid shelf traits, pore-surface bruising, and hardwood substrate.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Ganoderma applanatum
use_links.notes: Pored perennial conk morphology and brown to orangish-brown spore deposit.

records.look_alike_notes: Compare with birch polypore and hoof/tinder conk; artist's conk has a white pore surface that bruises/scratches brown.
records.source_note: Spore deposit corrected in v4.3.209: spores are released through the pore surface and form a brown to orangish-brown deposit.

records.notes: Look for pale hoof-like brackets on birch with a fine white pore surface underneath.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea/decoction and historical medicinal-use interest; use conservatively and do not treat as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Not a table mushroom; corky, bitter, and tough.
records.edibility_detail: Generally treated as non-culinary; better known for utility and tea-type use than as food.
records.general_notes: A birch specialist and a good host-linked ID species for the app.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Fruiting bodies persist and can be found year-round on birch.

records.other_uses: Also known historically as razor-strop fungus and as an ember/tinder material.
records.field_identification: Whitish to tan annual bracket on birch, often with a rounded inrolled margin and sunken pore surface.
records.mushroom_card_note: A birch-only polypore with traditional tea/tinder/utility interest, not a frying-pan mushroom.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Fomitopsis betulina
use_links.notes: Birch host, annual bracket form, and pore surface traits.

use_links.label: Research review: Fomitopsis betulina bioactive compounds
use_links.notes: Included as research/background, not as medical advice.

records.look_alike_notes: Birch host is the big clue. Artist's conk has a brown-bruising white pore surface and hoof fungus is harder/hoof-shaped and perennial.
records.source_note: Route cleanup: birch polypore is retained as traditional tea/utility/other-use material, not a normal edible mushroom target.

edible_use.notes: Traditional tea/decoction use only; not a normal edible/table mushroom.

records.notes: Irregular black charcoal-like mass on living birch with warm brown interior.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal and modern research interest, mostly as extracts/tea; evidence and safety vary, and it should not be treated as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Not a table mushroom. Traditionally simmered or steeped as a medicinal/traditional tea or decoction; treat as medicinal use, not a normal food mushroom.
records.non_edible_severity: Not a table mushroom / medicinal tea caution
records.edibility_detail: Medicinal/traditional tea use only; not a normal edible/table mushroom.
records.general_notes: Not a culinary mushroom; file under medicinal/traditional tea and other uses rather than normal edible mushrooms.
medicinalTerms: Kidney injury risk with heavy use

growth_form: Sterile conk / black mass on birch
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round on living birch trunks.

records.other_uses: Excellent practical tinder / ember carrier. The dry rusty-brown inner material catches a spark readily, smolders like a coal, and can help start or carry fire; scrape, crumble, or powder the inner material. If damp or freshly harvested, expose or dry the inner material first for best spark-catching.

medicinal.summary: Traditional Chaga tea/decoction/supplement use; not a food recommendation or treatment claim.
medical_terms: Kidney injury risk with heavy use
medicinal.preparation_notes: Usually simmered or steeped as tea/decoction; stronger extracts/supplements are a different risk category than occasional beverage-style use.
medicinal.warnings: Use moderation. Chaga can be high in oxalates and case reports link heavy or long-term powder use with kidney injury. It may also increase bleeding risk with anticoagulant/antiplatelet drugs and may affect blood sugar, so avoid casual supplement-style use with kidney disease, bleeding disorders, diabetes drugs, blood thinners, pregnancy, or before surgery without medical guidance.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Inonotus obliquus
use_links.notes: Black sterile conk on birch and sterile/fertile body distinction.

use_links.label: Review article: Inonotus obliquus research background
use_links.notes: Research/background link only; not a treatment recommendation.

use_links.label: Memorial Sloan Kettering: Chaga Mushroom
use_links.notes: Frames chaga as folk medicine/supplement material and notes safety concerns including bleeding, blood-sugar, and kidney/oxalate risks.

records.field_identification: Black, cracked, charcoal-like sterile conk protruding from living birch; interior is orange-brown, not wood.
records.look_alike_notes: Birch polypore and hoof fungus form recognizable brackets; chaga is a black, cracked sterile mass with orange-brown interior, usually on living birch.
records.edibility_notes: Medicinal/traditional tea use only; not a normal edible/table mushroom.

edible_use.notes: Traditionally prepared as tea/decoction; treated here as medicinal/traditional use rather than normal food.

records.mushroom_card_note: Birch conk used for medicinal/traditional tea/decoction; not a normal edible mushroom. Use caution with chronic/heavy use and medication or kidney concerns.

records.notes: Bright orange and yellow shelving bracket with pores below, not true gills.
records.culinary_uses: Young, tender shelves are a good edible mushroom after thorough cooking. Trim away tough bases and avoid old shelves; some people get stomach upset even from correctly identified chicken of the woods.
records.general_notes: Some people react to it, especially older specimens or those from some conifers.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young and tender, but not universally tolerated; avoid old, tough, bug-ridden, or conifer-grown material unless you know the local Laetiporus species well.

mushroom_profile.season_note: May through November in eastern hardwood records, but most typical in summer and fall; early spring finds are less common.

records.field_identification: Bright orange to yellow overlapping shelves growing from wood, with a pore surface underneath rather than gills. The texture is soft and fleshy when young, becoming tough with age; host tree and pore color help separate Laetiporus species.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good edible when young and cooked, but tolerance varies; confirm pores, wood growth, and host tree.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Laetiporus sulphureus
use_links.notes: Sulphur shelf field traits, pore surface, host/substrate notes, and edibility caution.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Laetiporus overview
use_links.notes: Separates common Laetiporus species and host patterns.

use_links.label: Tom Volk / UW-La Crosse: Laetiporus cincinnatus and chicken-of-the-woods
use_links.notes: Supports edible use when correctly identified and well cooked; raw-use stomach-upset risk; young tender/growing-edge quality; and Laetiporus species/host distinctions.

records.quality_notes: Young tender margins are best; older shelves become chalky, woody, sour, or bug-riddled.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook thoroughly. Use young tender portions. Try a small first serving.
records.risk_reason: Great Lakes Laetiporus species are not all the same. Conifer-associated forms are more often linked with bad reactions than classic hardwood chicken of the woods.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract and possible neurologic/systemic symptoms in sensitive people; vomiting, chills, and unusual reactions have been reported.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Bright orange/yellow shelf mushroom on living or dead trees; host tree matters. Conifer or hemlock growth in the Great Lakes should be higher caution than classic hardwood growth.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat old, tough, buggy, or conifer-growing chicken of the woods. For confirmed young hardwood specimens, eat a small amount first.
records.caution_reason: Substrate/species and individual-tolerance risk; high food value does not make every Laetiporus safe for every person.

lookalike_risk.label: High — orange wood mushroom and substrate/species caution
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — orange wood mushroom and substrate/species caution
lookalike_risk.summary: Chicken of the woods has pores, not gills. Risk includes poisonous orange gilled clusters and old/conifer/atypical Laetiporus tolerance issues.
required_checks: Reject gills, old/tough/sour/bug-ridden material, and uncertain conifer-associated specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: If it has gills, it is not chicken of the woods.

target_traits: Bright orange/yellow shelves with pore surface underneath
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it has gills, it is not chicken of the woods.

target_traits: Young tender hardwood shelves when target fits
lookalike_traits: Old, chalky, sour, bug-ridden, conifer-associated, or atypical material
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Old, tough, sour, bug-ridden, or uncertain-substrate Laetiporus stays out of the pan.

source_review_notes: Route remains edible-with-caution/prep-required. MushroomExpert links support ID/comparison and Laetiporus species context.

Tom Volk / UW-La Crosse supports chicken-of-the-woods as edible when the right young/tender portions are used and properly cooked; it also supports raw-use stomach-upset risk, species/host distinctions, and older or non-target portions being poor eating.

records.notes: Large spring shelf polypore on hardwoods with tan cap, brown flattened scales, angular pores running down the stem, and a mealy/cucumber-like odor. Use only tender young edges.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when very young and tender; older caps become leathery. Good sliced thin, sautéed, or dried for stock when still fragrant.
records.edibility_detail: Edible young only; quality drops quickly with age.
records.general_notes: Mostly worth collecting young; older specimens get leathery fast.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Most useful as a young spring mushroom before it toughens.

records.field_identification: Large scaly tan-to-brown shelf with big angular pores and a watermelon-rind/cucumber-like odor.
records.mushroom_card_note: Spring polypore; edible only when young and tender. Older shelves are tough.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Cerioporus squamosus
use_links.notes: Scaly cap, large angular pores, cucumber/watermelon odor, and hardwood habitat.

use_links.label: NPS: Dryad's saddle overview
use_links.notes: Public-agency overview supporting field recognition, ecology, and the edible-when-very-young / older-shelves-too-tough framing.

records.look_alike_notes: Older dryad's saddle becomes leathery; birch polypore and artist's conk are tougher shelf fungi without the big scaly cap and cucumber/watermelon odor.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — quality and bracket-polypore confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Dryad’s saddle is mainly an age/texture and bracket-polypore confusion issue, not a deadly look-alike problem.
required_checks: Check scaly cap and cucumber/watermelon-rind odor
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Gills, teeth, or old leathery shelves mean not eating-quality dryad’s saddle.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Teeth under a cap = not dryad’s saddle.

lookalike_traits: Large, leathery, tough, bug-ridden, woody flesh
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Old tough shelves are not eating-quality material.

source_review_notes: Current source mix is acceptable for the young-only/fair-quality food concept; v4.3.179 only clarified the NPS source note.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional reishi-style use; this guide should present this as traditional/field-guide use rather than medical treatment.
records.culinary_uses: Not eaten as a mushroom. Usually dried for tea/tincture by people who already know what they are doing.
records.edibility_detail: Not a food mushroom. Used in some traditional/reishi-style preparations, but this guide treats it as a cautious medicinal/practical ID entry, not a meal.
records.general_notes: Host matters: Ganoderma tsugae is strongly associated with hemlock/conifer wood, separating it from some hardwood Ganoderma look-alikes.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Annual bracket; most noticeable summer into fall.

records.field_identification: Glossy varnished red to reddish-brown shelf or kidney-shaped bracket on hemlock or other conifer wood. White pore surface bruises brown; flesh is corky/woody, not a tender eating mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Glossy red varnished shelf on hemlock; not food, traditional-use only.

edible_use.notes: Not a food mushroom. Used in some traditional/reishi-style preparations, but this guide treats it as a cautious medicinal/practical ID entry, not a meal.

records.notes: Large clustered rosette of overlapping fronds at the base of oaks and other hardwoods.
records.medicinal_uses: Maitake has supplement interest, but this guide treats wild hens mainly as a prime food mushroom.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible. Trim away tough bases, clean the layered fronds well, and cook; useful sautéed, roasted, dried, or in soups and gravies.
records.general_notes: A good host-linked species and one of the better fall edible clusters.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, young, and confidently identified; older rosettes get tough or bug-ridden.

staining: Blackens when old or bruised slightly
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly fall, especially at oak bases.

records.field_identification: Large gray-brown rosette of many overlapping spoon- or fan-shaped fronds, usually at the base of oak or other hardwoods. The underside has pores, not gills, and the fruitbody arises from a branching base.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice fall edible at oak bases; look for a big layered rosette with pored undersides.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Grifola frondosa
use_links.notes: Hen-of-the-woods rosette form, pored underside, hardwood-base habitat, and comparison notes.

use_links.label: Missouri Department of Conservation: Hen of the Woods / Maitake
use_links.notes: Supports ID traits, oak-base habitat, choice edible status, fresh/tender portion selection, slow cooking until tender, first-serving caution, and comparison with black-staining polypore, Berkeley’s polypore, and eastern cauliflower mushroom.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — polypore/quality confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: No major deadly look-alike is flagged, but hen can be confused with other rosette polypores and quality declines with age.
required_checks: Reject black-staining or thick cream shelf forms as hen
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Black-staining fronds, thick cream shelves, or ribbon folds are not hen of the woods.

target_traits: Gray-brown rosette of many spoon/fan fronds at oak base
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Black-staining fronds are not hen of the woods.
Thick cream shelves are not maitake.

lookalike_traits: Individual funnel-like caps on branching stalks
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Funnel caps on branching stalks key to umbrella polypore, not hen.

source_review_notes: Route remains edible-with-confident-ID. MushroomExpert supports ID/comparison, including rosette form, pored underside, hardwood-base habitat, and lookalike context.

Missouri Department of Conservation supports edible-use framing, choice-edible quality, fresh/tender portion selection, cooking until tender, first-serving caution, and comparison with black-staining polypore, Berkeley’s polypore, and eastern cauliflower mushroom. Do not import MDC medicinal-health claims into this food record.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Not edible as food. Keep as an ID/practical-use bracket fungus.
records.edibility_detail: Not edible as food; hard, perennial, woody bracket. Keep as an identification/practical-use record only. North American red-belted-conk material may represent Fomitopsis mounceae/schrenkii-style members of the former F. pinicola complex rather than strict Eurasian F. pinicola, so this guide treats the name as a cautious group label.
records.general_notes: Common northern conk. Compare with artist’s conk and hemlock varnish shelf; red-belted conk lacks the glossy varnished reishi surface and has a distinct banded hoof/shelf form.

edible_use.notes: Not a food mushroom; tough woody conk.

images_structured.title: Red-belted conk field view
images_structured.author: George Chernilevsky
images_structured.rights_notes: Exact author/license metadata copied from local credit table (credits-enrichment-v109); source page retained for attribution.

records.field_identification: Hard woody perennial bracket with a pale growing margin, darker older zones, and often a reddish-orange band near the edge. Underside is pored and pale; grows on dead conifer or hardwood logs, stumps, and snags.
records.mushroom_card_note: Hard banded conk with red belt; useful ID/practical species, not food.
records.other_uses: Can be used as a teaching/display bracket. Some traditional tinder/practical interest exists for bracket fungi, but this guide treats it mainly as a woody non-food ID entry.
records.taxonomy_notes: North American red-belted conks have been split from strict Eurasian Fomitopsis pinicola in modern treatments; use this guide record as a practical field group, not a final microscope/DNA-level determination.

records.notes: Hard hoof-shaped bracket on hardwoods, especially birch and beech. The tough gray-brown hoof form and pale pore underside are the key field marks.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.general_notes: Not food; included for tinder/utility value and for separating woody conks from edible shelf fungi.

images_structured.title: Tinder conk / hoof fungus on wood

records.photo_review_cleanup_note: Removed from Needs Review because the only explicit review reasons were image/photo-related and a real Commons-backed image is now available.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional and supplement interest, especially as tea/extract; this guide does not treat it as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Too tough for table eating; usually dried and simmered for tea/decoction.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Thin leathery shelf with strongly zoned, fuzzy cap surface and a true tiny-pored white underside.
records.mushroom_card_note: Tea/decoction mushroom, not table food; confirm true pores underneath.

records.edibility_detail: Edible but uncommon; this guide should emphasize conservation-minded foraging and strong ID before harvest.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when confidently identified and fresh, but harvest conservatively; if only one fruiting body is found, leave it or document it rather than taking it.
records.overview: Important MAMI-listed species and useful look-alike/comparison entry for hen-of-the-woods style clusters, but should be treated conservatively because it is not commonly encountered.
records.notes: Important MAMI-listed species and useful look-alike/comparison entry for hen-of-the-woods style clusters, but should be treated conservatively because it is not commonly encountered.
records.general_notes: Important MAMI-listed species and useful look-alike/comparison entry for hen-of-the-woods style clusters, but should be treated conservatively because it is not commonly encountered.
records.field_identification: Compound cluster with many small round umbrella-like caps branching from a central/base stalk; pores, not gills; fruits on the ground near hardwood roots, stumps, or buried wood. Reported as uncommon/rare in North America.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Polyporus umbellatus
use_links.notes: Fruits terrestrially near hardwood bases/roots; rare in North America.

use_links.label: MAMI: Polyporus umbellatus
use_links.notes: Dimensions and identifying characteristics for umbrella polypore.

records.edibility_detail: Edible when young and tender, but commonly tough or leathery. Use only tender margins or use for broth/duxelles-style preparations after confident identification.
records.culinary_uses: Niche edible. Best treated as a tough, flavorful polypore: trim tender young margins, cook thoroughly, or use for stock/broth rather than expecting hen-of-the-woods texture.
records.other_uses: Useful as a comparison species for hen-of-the-woods-style rosettes at hardwood bases.
records.overview: Large rosette-forming hardwood-base polypore that bruises dark brown to black, especially on pore surface, margins, and damaged flesh.
records.notes: Hen-of-the-woods look-alike with a key difference: fresh tissue and pore surfaces darken brown to black when bruised. Often tougher than hen of the woods.
records.general_notes: Added to resolve look-alike references from Hen of the woods and related polypore records.
records.field_identification: Large overlapping rosette or clustered fan-shaped caps at or near hardwood bases, especially oak/root/stump zones. Underside has pores, not gills. Fresh pore surface, cap margin, and cut or bruised flesh stain dark brown to black.
records.habitat: At bases of hardwoods, stumps, buried roots, and root zones; especially around oak and other hardwoods.

flesh_color: White, staining dark when bruised or cut
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer into fall; often July through November.

records.mushroom_card_note: Hen-like hardwood-base rosette, but bruises black and is usually tougher.

images_structured.author: Katja Schulz
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required when author metadata is available.
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required when author metadata is available.

images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required when author metadata is available.

records.caution_reason: Young tender margins only; commonly tough; confirm the hardwood-base polypore carefully.

edible_use.notes: Use young tender margins or broth/stock preparations after confident identification; older material is commonly tough.

records.food_quality: Source-limited; edible young/tender by some accounts
records.edibility_detail: Source-limited edible/caution entry. Young tender edges are reported as edible by some foragers after cooking, but older fruiting bodies are often tough or bitter. Do not treat as source-cleared until direct edible-use support is attached.
records.culinary_uses: Young tender edges may be cooked after confident ID, but older fruiting bodies are usually tough.
records.overview: Large pale rosette/shelf polypore at the bases of hardwoods, especially oaks.
records.notes: Giant hardwood-base polypore that can be confused with hen of the woods or black-staining polypore. Does not have the same black-staining reaction as Meripilus sumstinei.
records.general_notes: Source/status cleanup v4.3.170: MushroomExpert is added as ID/ecology support only; edible-use source support still needed.
records.field_identification: Very large overlapping fan or shelf-like caps at hardwood bases or buried roots. Whitish to cream/tan cap surfaces, white pore surface, tough flesh that becomes bitter/tough with age.
records.habitat: At bases of oaks and other hardwoods, around stumps and buried roots.

bruising: Does not characteristically black-stain like Meripilus

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Bondarzewia berkeleyi
use_links.notes: ID/ecology support for giant hardwood-base rosettes, pore surface, thick white flesh, eastern North America range, and summer/fall fruiting. Does not clear edible-use status.

records.mushroom_card_note: Huge pale hardwood-base polypore; compare carefully with hen and black-staining polypore.

images_structured.title: Berkeley's polypore rosette
images_structured.author: Jason Hollinger
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required when author metadata is available.
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required when author metadata is available.

edible_use.method: Cooked food / young tender portions only
edible_use.notes: Young tender edges only after confident ID; older fruiting bodies are usually tough or bitter. Edible-use source review remains pending.
